beginner.question: What are the main components of a CSS rule?
beginner.answer: A CSS rule consists of three main components: a selector (to target HTML elements), a declaration block enclosed in curly braces {}, and individual declarations inside the block. Each declaration has a property and a value separated by a colon, ending with a semicolon. Example: selector { property: value; }

beginner.question: What is the purpose of the semicolon in CSS?
beginner.answer: The semicolon separates declarations within a declaration block. Each declaration must end with a semicolon to indicate where one property-value pair ends and the next begins. Without semicolons, CSS cannot distinguish between separate declarations, causing syntax errors. The last declaration's semicolon is technically optional but best practice is to always include it.

beginner.question: What is the difference between a property and a value in CSS?
beginner.answer: A property is the aspect of an element you want to style (such as color, font-size, margin, or background), while a value is the specific setting you apply to that property (such as red, 16px, 10px, or #ffffff). The property and value are separated by a colon: property: value.

beginner.question: How do you group multiple selectors that share the same styles?
beginner.answer: Multiple selectors are grouped using commas. Example: h1, h2, h3 { font-family: Arial, sans-serif; color: #333; } This applies the same styles to all h1, h2, and h3 elements. Grouping reduces code repetition and improves maintainability.

beginner.question: What is the correct spacing and formatting convention for CSS?
beginner.answer: Industry convention includes: one declaration per line, one level of indentation (2 spaces or 1 tab) per property, a space after the colon, and the closing brace on its own line. While CSS ignores most whitespace, consistent formatting improves readability and maintainability.

beginner.question: What does the !important annotation do in CSS?
beginner.answer: The !important annotation gives a declaration the highest priority, overriding normal cascade rules regardless of specificity or source order. Example: color: red !important; It should be used sparingly as it breaks the natural cascade and makes debugging difficult.

beginner.question: What is a shorthand property in CSS? Give an example.
beginner.answer: A shorthand property combines multiple individual properties into a single declaration. For example, border: 1px solid black; combines border-width, border-style, and border-color. Similarly, margin: 10px 20px; combines margin-top, margin-right, margin-bottom, and margin-left into one line.

beginner.question: When must you use quotes in CSS?
beginner.answer: Quotes are required for: (1) string values in the content property (content: "Hello"); (2) font family names containing spaces (font-family: "Times New Roman"); and (3) URLs in url() (background-image: url("image.jpg")). Single or double quotes both work as long as they match.

beginner.question: Are CSS property names case-sensitive?
beginner.answer: CSS is generally case-insensitive for property names and most values — BACKGROUND-COLOR, background-color, and Background-Color all work. However, best practice is always lowercase. CSS is case-sensitive for IDs (#myID), class names, URLs/file paths (depending on the filesystem), and some font names.

beginner.question: What happens to the style if you forget to close a curly brace in CSS?
beginner.answer: If you forget a closing curly brace, the browser treats everything after that point as part of the unclosed rule, effectively breaking all subsequent CSS until the next closing brace is encountered or the stylesheet ends. This is one of the most common and impactful CSS syntax errors.

intermediate.question: Explain the difference between margin shorthand with 1, 2, 3, and 4 values.
intermediate.answer: 1 value (margin: 10px) applies to all four sides. 2 values (margin: 10px 20px) set top/bottom to 10px and left/right to 20px. 3 values (margin: 10px 20px 30px) set top to 10px, left/right to 20px, and bottom to 30px. 4 values (margin: 10px 20px 30px 40px) apply clockwise: top, right, bottom, left.

intermediate.question: What is the order of values in the font shorthand property?
intermediate.answer: The font shorthand order is: font-style, font-variant, font-weight, font-size/line-height, font-family. Example: font: italic bold 16px/1.5 'Arial', sans-serif; Only font-size and font-family are required; the rest are optional. Font-size must precede font-family, separated by a slash for line-height.

intermediate.question: How does CSS handle vendor prefixes and why are they needed?
intermediate.answer: Vendor prefixes (-webkit-, -moz-, -ms-, -o-) are used for experimental, non-standardized CSS features before they become official. They allow browser vendors to implement new properties while the specification is still being developed. The standard unprefixed version should always be placed last so it takes precedence once implemented. Autoprefixer can automate adding/removing prefixes.

intermediate.question: What is the risk of using shorthand properties for values you don't intend to set?
intermediate.answer: Shorthand properties reset any omitted sub-properties to their initial values. For example, background: url(image.jpg) not only sets the background-image but also resets background-color, background-repeat, background-position, etc. to their defaults. This can unintentionally override previously set sub-properties.

intermediate.question: Why is the semicolon after the last declaration in a block considered a best practice?
intermediate.answer: Including a semicolon after the last declaration is a best practice because it prevents errors when adding new declarations later — you won't forget to add a semicolon to the previously-last declaration. It also makes version control diffs cleaner (adding a new line doesn't require modifying the preceding line).

intermediate.question: What syntax error makes CSS skip an entire rule vs skipping just one declaration?
intermediate.answer: If the selector syntax is invalid or the opening brace is missing, the entire rule is skipped. If a property name is invalid or a value is incorrect for its property, only that single declaration is skipped — the rest of the rule still applies. However, missing a closing brace causes everything after it until the next accidental closing brace to fail.

intermediate.question: How does the browser handle invalid CSS syntax?
intermediate.answer: Browsers are forgiving with CSS syntax errors — they follow the 'ignore rule' principle: invalid declarations are ignored while valid ones still apply. Invalid selectors cause the entire rule block to be ignored. Invalid property values cause only that declaration to be ignored. This graceful degradation ensures one syntax error doesn't break the entire stylesheet.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between using `url()` with and without quotes?
intermediate.answer: In CSS, url() can be used with or without quotes: url(image.jpg) and url("image.jpg") are both valid. However, quotes are recommended for consistency and to handle special characters (spaces, parentheses, etc.) in the URL path. Some older browsers may have issues with unquoted URLs containing special characters.

intermediate.question: How do you write valid CSS for the content property with special characters?
intermediate.answer: Special characters in the content property can be represented using escaped Unicode: content: "\00A9"; for the copyright symbol, or content: "\2192"; for an arrow. You can also paste actual characters if the file encoding supports them (UTF-8 recommended). CSS escapes use a backslash followed by the hexadecimal Unicode code point.

intermediate.question: What happens when you have a space between a number and its unit in CSS?
intermediate.answer: A space between a number and its unit (e.g., margin: 10 px;) makes the value invalid. CSS requires values like 10px, 2em, 50% to have no whitespace between the numeric value and the unit identifier. The browser will ignore the invalid declaration, potentially causing layout issues.

advanced.question: Explain the exact CSS parsing algorithm and how tokenization works.
advanced.answer: CSS parsing follows the CSS Syntax specification: first, the stylesheet is tokenized into a stream of tokens (identifiers, hashes, strings, functions, numbers, etc.). The tokenizer handles escape sequences, comments (which are replaced with whitespace), and character encoding. Then the parser processes tokens into rules (qualified rules and at-rules) per the CSS grammar. Invalid tokens trigger error recovery — the parser discards input until it finds a valid recovery point.

advanced.question: How does the CSS parser handle at-rules like @media, @keyframes, and @font-face differently from regular rules?
advanced.answer: At-rules start with an @ token followed by an identifier and have their own parsing rules. @media contains nested rules inside a block. @keyframes contains a block with keyframe selectors and declaration blocks. @font-face contains declarations at the top level of its block. Each at-rule type has its own grammar defined in the CSS specifications. Invalid at-rules are discarded entirely, while valid ones with invalid content may have partial error recovery.

advanced.question: What is the CSS value definition syntax and how do you read it?
advanced.answer: The CSS value definition syntax (used in specifications) uses symbols to describe valid values. '<length>' means a length data type. '|' separates alternatives. '||' means at least one must appear in any order. '&&' means all must appear in any order. '?' means optional. '*' means zero or more. '+' means one or more. '{}' indicates occurrences. For example, 'margin: <length>{1,4}' means 1 to 4 length values.

advanced.question: How does the CSS cascading order resolve when multiple stylesheets have rules with the same specificity?
advanced.answer: When specificity is equal, the cascade uses source order: later declarations override earlier ones. Source order is determined by: (1) the order of @import statements; (2) the order of <link> tags in HTML; (3) the order of rules within a stylesheet; (4) the order of <style> blocks. Rules imported later or appearing later in the document override earlier ones with equal specificity.

advanced.question: Explain the concept of 'CSS-wide keywords' and how they differ from property-specific values.
advanced.answer: CSS-wide keywords are special values that can be used with any CSS property: 'initial' (property's specified default), 'inherit' (parent's computed value), 'unset' (inherit for inherited properties, initial for others), 'revert' (cascade origin default), and 'revert-layer' (previous cascade layer value). They override property-specific values and are handled at the cascade level rather than the property level.

advanced.question: What are the component value types in CSS tokenization and how do they affect parsing?
advanced.answer: CSS tokenization produces component values of several types: identifiers (auto, block), strings ("quoted"), URLs (url()), numbers (42), dimensions (16px, 2em), percentages (50%), hashes (#ff0000), functions (rgb(), calc()), operators (+, -, /, *), and delimiters (;, :, {, }, (, )). The parser groups these into functional blocks by matching opening/closing tokens (parentheses, brackets, braces). Mismatched grouping causes parse errors.

advanced.question: How does CSS handle property declaration conflicts when using the 'all' shorthand?
advanced.answer: The 'all' shorthand resets all properties except direction and unicode-bidi to a specific CSS-wide keyword (initial, inherit, unset, or revert). For example, 'all: unset' resets every property to either inherit or initial depending on the property's default inheritance behavior. When 'all' conflicts with other declarations, the cascade resolution follows normal rules — the winning declaration is determined by origin, specificity, and source order.

advanced.question: What is the difference between CSS grammar errors and CSS constraint errors? Provide examples.
advanced.answer: Grammar errors violate the syntactic structure of CSS (missing closing brace, invalid selector syntax) and typically cause the browser to skip whole rules. Constraint errors occur when the syntax is valid but the values violate constraints (a negative width, a color value exceeding the range, a font-weight of 5000). Modern CSS parsers handle grammar errors at parse time and constraint errors during computed value processing, with different recovery behaviors.

advanced.question: How do CSS custom properties interact with the syntax for property values that accept multiple comma-separated items?
advanced.answer: CSS custom properties can contain commas since they store the entire value as a string. When used with var(), the entire substituted value must be syntactically valid for the property. For example, --shadows: 2px 2px 0 #000, 1px 1px 0 #333; then text-shadow: var(--shadows); works because the full substituted value (two shadows) is valid. However, substituting a partial value like var(--color), #333 as a fallback for a single-color property fails because commas split declaration values.

advanced.question: How does the CSS parser's error recovery differ for qualified rules versus at-rules?
advanced.answer: For qualified rules (standard selectors with declaration blocks), error recovery discards the invalid selector but keeps valid declarations if possible — the parser re-synchronizes at the next { or } token. For at-rules, error recovery depends on the at-rule type: unknown at-rules are discarded entirely (including their block), while known at-rules like @media may recover by skipping invalid rules inside their block and continuing to parse valid ones. Both types use the concept of 'parse errors' which must be ignored without affecting surrounding valid CSS.
